rationale: All spot-checked citations resolve correctly: Angirasa Smṛti p-07cb57 contains the exact dharma-as-codanā definition cited; Manusmṛti p-f0b26c (Mn_1.87) confirms varna-duty passage; Fichte ch.2 p-3c8e3d and p-b77ba3 substantiate the commanded-vs-permitted moral law distinction; Charles Comte ch.18 p-06a027 and ch.20 p-e4a7c9 confirm the moral-law-vs-civil-law framework. The comparative synthesis is well-reasoned and grounded in real corpus text, with no fabrications detected.
verifications_performed: Pulled Angirasa Smṛti ch.1 p-07cb57 — confirmed 'Dharma is said to be that which is a scriptural injunction (codanā)' verbatim
Pulled Manusmṛti ch.1 p-f0b26c — confirmed Mn_1.87 varna-duties passage (protection of creation, duties assigned to those born from mouth/arms/thighs/feet)
Pulled Fichte ch.2 p-e62dae — confirmed moral law / personhood / freedom passage; answer's characterization is accurate
Pulled Fichte ch.2 p-3c8e3d and p-b77ba3 — confirmed commanded-vs-permitted moral law distinction with inalienability argument
Pulled Charles Comte ch.18 p-06a027 — confirmed moral laws as forces inherent in human nature without government intervention
Pulled Charles Comte ch.20 p-e4a7c9 — confirmed moral laws extend further than civil laws (legislation less extensive than morality)
